product_description: Sticklight's production-grade product for converting, hosting, and delivering AI-built apps. Designed for builders coming from tools like Lovable, Cursor, and Bolt.
clash_notes: Workspace means the account/team container in Pink — NOT the creation start screen (which is the Sticklight builder meaning)
Project means the managed unit being converted, hosted, and deployed in Pink — NOT a saved builder output (which is the Sticklight builder meaning)
Build is a specific technical pipeline step name in Pink — NOT flexible supporting copy alongside Create (which is the Sticklight builder meaning)

terms.definition: Sticklight's production-grade product for converting, hosting, and delivering AI-built apps. Pink is designed for builders coming from tools like Lovable, Cursor, and Bolt. It handles conversion, hosting, and full-stack setup in one place so users don't need to assemble production infrastructure themselves.

when_to_use: When referring to the Pink product or product area
When describing the production-ready next step after building an app elsewhere
In product, onboarding, and positioning copy
when_not_to_use: When referring generally to Sticklight as a whole
When referring to a specific project, environment, or deployment
As a generic term for hosting or infrastructure
notes: Treat Pink as a product name
Use it when the product context matters, not as a substitute for technical terms like deployment or environment

examples.correct: Pink helps you take AI-built apps live in one place.

avoid: Project (when referring to the higher-level container)
terms.definition: The shared container where users manage projects, environments, team settings, and billing in Pink. A workspace groups the people, projects, and operational settings that belong together.
terms.clash_warning: In the Sticklight builder, Workspace means the creation start screen. In Pink, Workspace means the account/team container. These are different concepts. Be explicit in documentation when both products appear together.

when_to_use: When referring to the account-level or team-level area in Pink
In navigation, settings, billing, and team management copy
When describing where multiple projects are organized
when_not_to_use: When referring to a single project
When referring to the project creation wizard
When referring to the Sticklight builder start screen
notes: In Pink, Workspace means the account/container layer
This is different from the main Sticklight builder use of Workspace as the project-starting surface
Be explicit in documentation when both products appear together

correct: Manage team access from your Workspace settings.
examples.avoid: Open the workspace to start prompting your site.

avoid: App (when referring to the managed unit in Pink)
Site (when referring to the Pink-managed unit)
terms.definition: The main unit users create and manage in Pink. A project represents the app being converted, hosted, configured, and deployed. It contains its environments, deployments, domains, and settings.
terms.clash_warning: In the Sticklight builder, Project means a saved digital product (website, app, store). In Pink, Project means the managed container for one app being hosted and deployed. The term overlaps but the context is different.

when_to_use: When referring to the main managed item in Pink
In setup flows, settings, deployments, and environments
When describing the user's app as it exists inside Pink
when_not_to_use: When referring specifically to the source code repository
When referring specifically to one environment
When referring to the workspace that contains multiple projects
notes: A project is the managed container for one app in Pink
A project can have multiple environments and deployments

terms.definition: The starting point Pink uses to create a new project. A source can be a connected Git provider or a pasted repository URL.

when_to_use: In the first step of project setup
When referring to where Pink gets the app from
When describing project creation and import flows
when_not_to_use: When referring specifically to a Git repository — use repository
When referring to source code in general
When referring to build output or deployed infrastructure
notes: Source is the broader concept
Repository is the specific code location users connect or paste
Use Source as the step name; use repository in supporting explanation

correct: Choose a source to get started.
Connect your Git account or paste a repository URL as the source.

avoid: Repo (in user-facing copy unless space is limited)
Codebase (when referring to the connected Git location)
Project (when referring specifically to the Git location)
terms.definition: The Git repository Pink connects to in order to analyze, build, and deploy a project. A repository contains the project's source code and is typically connected through GitHub or provided via URL.

when_to_use: When referring to the connected code repository
In setup flows, build flows, and settings
When users connect GitHub or paste a repository URL
when_not_to_use: When referring to the Pink project as a whole
When referring to a deployment or environment
When referring generally to source input — use source
notes: Use repository in explanatory copy
Repo may be acceptable in highly technical, space-limited surfaces only
Keep the distinction clear: repository = code source; project = managed app in Pink

correct: Connect a repository to create your project.

avoid: Production deployment (when referring to the environment itself)
Main environment (unless officially named that way)
terms.definition: The live environment used to run the production version of a project. The production environment includes the connected resources, variables, and domain configuration used by the live app.

when_to_use: When referring specifically to the live environment
In environment pages, deployment flows, and operational copy
When users configure production resources or variables
when_not_to_use: When referring to the live status badge alone
When referring to a single release event — use deployment
When referring to the whole project
notes: Use production environment for the configured live runtime context
Distinguish from deployment, which is the act/result of releasing code

correct: Connected resources and custom variables for this environment.

avoid: Release (unless intentionally used in a broader product narrative)
Go live (as the primary system term)
Build (when referring to the delivered result)
terms.definition: A release of a project to an environment in Pink. A deployment is the result of Pink building and delivering a version of the project so it can run in its target environment.

when_to_use: When referring to release history or operational status
In navigation, deployment logs, and redeploy actions
When describing the act of delivering an updated version of a project
when_not_to_use: When referring to the project itself
When referring to the build process alone
When referring to the environment that receives the deployment
notes: Use deployment as the primary production term
Avoid publish here — Pink is an app hosting/deployment product, not a website publisher
A deployment happens to an environment

terms.definition: A user-owned domain connected to a Pink project. A custom domain lets users serve their project on their own branded web address instead of a platform-provided domain.

when_to_use: In domain setup and settings
When users connect or manage their own domain
When distinguishing platform domains from user-owned domains
when_not_to_use: When referring to the default platform domain
When referring generally to any URL without domain context
notes: Use custom domain when the branded, user-owned nature matters
Use domain only when the shorter term is sufficient and unambiguous

terms.definition: Connected services and external resources that provide functionality, data, or secrets to an environment. These can include databases, APIs, and other linked services needed for the project to run.

when_to_use: As the section label for linked services in an environment
When referring to managed external services and connected resources
In environment configuration flows
when_not_to_use: When referring only to variables — use custom variables
When referring only to user-facing integrations in the builder product
notes: Good umbrella term for infra-style connections
Avoid overusing integrations alone if the section also includes infrastructure resources like databases

correct: Managed and linked services that supply connection details or secrets.

avoid: Secrets (when referring to the whole set)
terms.definition: Project or environment-specific key-value variables used to configure how an app runs. Custom variables can include environment settings, API endpoints, and other configuration values.

when_to_use: When referring to user-added variables in the environment
In environment setup, import, and editing flows
When describing .env import or manual variable entry
when_not_to_use: When referring only to sensitive values — use secrets if that becomes a supported term
When referring generally to resources or integrations
notes: Use custom variables as the user-facing term
You can mention .env in supporting copy, but don't replace the main term with jargon
If Secrets is later introduced as a separate concept, define the distinction clearly

correct: Add custom variables for this environment.

avoid: Assemble (as the main system step name)
terms.definition: The step where Pink prepares the project for deployment. Build turns the connected repository into a runnable application artifact.
terms.clash_warning: In the Sticklight builder, 'build' is used flexibly as supporting copy alongside Create. In Pink, Build is a specific technical pipeline step name with a precise meaning. Keep these contexts distinct.

when_to_use: As a step in the project creation or deployment flow
When referring to the preparation phase before deployment
In build logs, progress, and troubleshooting
when_not_to_use: When referring to the final deployment
When referring to project creation more broadly
When referring to user-facing creation in the main builder product
notes: In Pink, build is an engineering/runtime term
Keep distinct from Create in the main Sticklight builder

correct: Pink builds the project before deployment.

terms.definition: The step where Pink examines the connected project before building it. Analyze helps Pink understand the project structure and prepare it for successful conversion and deployment.

when_to_use: As a project setup step
When referring to the evaluation phase between repository connection and build
In progress indicators and onboarding
when_not_to_use: When referring to security scans
When referring to user analytics or performance analysis
notes: Keep this distinct from Security scans or product analytics
Use simple, confidence-building language

correct: Pink analyzes the project before building it.

avoid: Rebuild (unless the action is technically only a rebuild)
Retry deployment (unless in a failure state)
terms.definition: An action that triggers a new deployment of an existing project. Redeploy is used when users want to deliver the project again, typically after changes or configuration updates.

when_to_use: As an action on an already deployed project
In environments and deployment flows
When users want to re-run delivery without creating a new project
when_not_to_use: When creating the first deployment
When only editing variables without yet triggering delivery
notes: Stronger and more accurate than publish in this context
Good action label for a production-grade hosting product

terms.definition: A status that indicates the project or environment is currently running and accessible.

when_to_use: As a short status badge
When indicating a project/environment is active and available
In status surfaces and environment headers
when_not_to_use: As a substitute for deployment or production environment
When referring to the act of going live
notes: Good as a compact status label
Keep it as a status, not as the name of a workflow or object
